nodes.identity_summary: P05_T020 is an ingredient-first, brand-loyal, and risk-averse consumer who deeply values natural, non-toxic formulations for their dry skin. They rely heavily on personal network recommendations and detailed online reviews for validation, while being skeptical of advertising and international brands. Their purchase decisions are methodical—prioritizing lightweight, moisturizing body washes within a mid-range budget—and they favor established Indian brands or trusted global names like Dove, which they've used since childhood.

nodes.label: ingredient-first-decision-maker
nodes.description: Ranks ingredients as the most important factor when buying personal care products, above brand, price, and recommendations.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] When buying any personal care product, rank these factors from most to least important: ['ingredients','brand','price','recommendation','expert','reviews','packaging','fragrance']
[REAL] When shopping online for personal care, what do you look at first? Pick your top 3: ['ingredients','review_count','brand_name']

[BRANCH] When browsing for a new body wash online, do you spend more time examining the product images and packaging design, or reading the detailed description, ingredients list, and customer reviews? I always skip straight to the ingredients list and reviews – that's what really matters for my skin.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a new body wash concept, this person would first scrutinize the ingredient list and reject products with ingredients they perceive as harmful or unnatural.

nodes.label: brand-loyal-repurchaser
nodes.description: Very loyal to personal care brands, typically repurchases the same body wash (Dove) and only switches when forced by external factors like a free sample or doctor's recommendation.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] How loyal are you to personal care brands? Very_loyal
[REAL] When your current body wash/soap runs out, what is your typical next step? Repurchase_same

[REAL] Describe the last time you tried a completely new body wash brand. What triggered the switch? Were you satisfied? Got it for free, otherwise loyal to Dove. Body wash doesn't give much satisfaction Ñ Dove is light on body and skin, been using since childhood
nodes.predictive_value: When exposed to a new brand launch, this person is likely to ignore it and continue purchasing their trusted Dove body wash unless presented with a compelling, risk-free trial.

nodes.label: price-sensitive-within-mid-range
nodes.description: Typically spends ₹200-350 on body wash, has paid up to ₹400 for a product deemed worth it, but is unwilling to pay a premium for values like sustainability or Ayurvedic ingredients.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] How much do you typically spend on a single body wash purchase? 200_350

[REAL] What is the maximum premium (%) you would pay for a product that aligns with your values? Rate each separately: {'Sustainable/eco-friendly packaging':'0','Ayurvedic or traditional ingredients':'0','Clinical-grade, lab-tested formulation':'0','Cruelty-free, not tested on animals':'0','Made in India':'0'}
[REAL] What, if anything, frustrates you or feels 'just okay' about your current body wash? Price is high
nodes.predictive_value: When comparing two similar body washes, this person will choose the one priced within their ₹200-350 comfort zone, even if the more expensive one has ethical or premium claims.

nodes.label: natural-toxic-free-seeker
nodes.description: Rates natural importance as 5 (very important) and defines natural as 'free of all toxics that will have carcinogenic effect on my face, body or skin.'
evidence_quotes: [REAL] How important is it to you that a product is 'natural' or 'chemical-free'? And what does 'natural' mean to you in your own words? {'rating':5,'explanation':'natural means it free of all toxics that will have carcinogenic effect on my face, body or skin'}
[REAL] Which certifications or labels influence your purchase? Select all that apply. Paraben_free,ph_balanced,sulfate_free,cruelty_free
[REAL] What claims or product descriptions on a body wash would make you stop and read more? Pick top 3: ['india_specific','intense_moisture','natural']
nodes.predictive_value: When shown an ad highlighting 'natural,' 'paraben-free,' or 'non-toxic' claims, this person will stop to read more and is more likely to consider the product.

nodes.label: trusts-personal-network-and-online-reviews
nodes.description: Trusts friends and family (rating 4) and online reviews on Amazon/Nykaa (rating 4) more than advertisements, influencers, or dermatologists for personal care information.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] How much do you trust the following sources for personal care product information? Rate each 1-5: {'TV advertisements':1,'Instagram or YouTube influencers':2,'Dermatologists or skin doctors':2,'Friends and family':4,'Online reviews (Amazon, Nykaa)':4,'Claims written on the product packaging':1}
[SYNTHETIC] You have 10 'trust points' to allocate across these sources when deciding on a new body wash: ... How do you distribute them? C) 3 points, D) 3 points, B) 2 points, A) 1 point, E) 1 point
[SYNTHETIC] If you had to allocate 100 points across these factors that influence your body wash choice... What online reviews say (40), What friends/family recommend (30)
nodes.predictive_value: A product with numerous positive reviews on Amazon/Nykaa or a recommendation from a close friend will significantly increase this person's purchase likelihood over a heavily advertised product.

nodes.label: prefers-Indian-brands-over-international
nodes.description: Believes Indian personal care brands are 'appropriately priced, easy to access and restock' and that international brands 'lack credibility.'
evidence_quotes: [REAL] How do you feel about Indian personal care brands vs. international brands? Do you have a preference or bias? Indian personal care brands are performing well Ñ appropriately priced, easy to access and restock. Don't enjoy international brands as they lack credibility

[SYNTHETIC] A body wash brand offers two identical products with different labels: one says 'Made in India' priced at ₹249, the other says 'Imported from Korea' priced at ₹449. Same size, similar ingredients. Which are you more likely to buy? Buy the 'Made in India' option at ₹249. No premium for imported product.
nodes.predictive_value: When choosing between an Indian and an international brand with similar claims, this person will lean toward the Indian brand, perceiving it as better value and more credible.

nodes.label: dry-skin-moisturization-seeker
nodes.description: Has dry skin and seeks body wash that moisturizes, with an ideal post-wash feeling being 'moisturized.'
evidence_quotes: [REAL] What specific skin concerns, if any, do you have? Select all that apply. Dryness
[REAL] What is your ideal post-wash skin feeling? Moisturized
[REAL] What specific benefits or features would you expect from a body wash designed for Indian skin and hard water? Lightweight, mild, rinses off easily, moisturizes, cleans, gentle on skin
nodes.predictive_value: When evaluating body wash claims, this person will be highly responsive to 'intense moisture,' 'hydrating,' or 'for dry skin' promises.

nodes.label: lightweight-texture-preference
nodes.description: Prefers lightweight texture in body wash, as noted in current body wash likes ('lightweight') and ideal formulation ('lightweight gentle formulation').
evidence_quotes: [REAL] What texture do you prefer in a body wash? Lightweight
[REAL] What do you like MOST about your current body wash? (Top 1-2 things) Lightweight, can use on face, cleans effectively, mild, been using since childhood
[REAL] If you could design your PERFECT body wash from scratch, describe it. ... lightweight gentle formulation
nodes.predictive_value: Will favor body washes described as 'lightweight,' 'gel-based,' or 'non-greasy' and may reject products marketed as rich creams or heavy oils.

nodes.label: research-oriented-before-purchase
nodes.description: Spends time reading ingredients, reviews, and comparing products across platforms before buying, especially online.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] When shopping online for personal care, what do you look at first? Pick your top 3: ['ingredients','review_count','brand_name']

[BRANCH] When browsing for a new body wash online, do you spend more time examining the product images and packaging design, or reading the detailed description, ingredients list, and customer reviews? I always skip straight to the ingredients list and reviews
[SYNTHETIC] Imagine you have 30 minutes before buying a body wash online. How would you split that time? Allocate 30 minutes across: ... Reading ingredient lists 12, reading text reviews 10
nodes.predictive_value: Will not make an impulse purchase; will dedicate time to cross-check ingredients and reviews on Amazon/Nykaa before deciding.

nodes.label: risk-averse-to-new-brands
nodes.description: Only switches body wash for a doctor's recommendation or free sample, and has been using Dove since childhood, indicating high reluctance to try unfamiliar brands.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] What would make you switch away from your current body wash? Pick your top 3: ['doctor_rec','free_sample']
[REAL] Describe the last time you tried a completely new body wash brand. What triggered the switch? Were you satisfied? Got it for free, otherwise loyal to Dove.

[SYNTHETIC] You've been using a ₹199 body wash for a year and are satisfied. A colleague at work casually mentions your body wash is 'the budget option' and recommends their ₹499 one. How does this affect your next purchase? Be curious and look up the ₹499 option online before deciding.
nodes.predictive_value: A new brand will struggle to gain trial without offering a risk-free sample or an endorsement from a trusted medical expert.

nodes.label: mood-dependent-grooming-routine
nodes.description: Relationship with bathing/grooming is 'Mood_dependent,' suggesting product use and attention to grooming vary with their emotional state.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] How would you describe your relationship with bathing/grooming? Mood_dependent

[BRANCH] How does your current mood or situation (e.g., feeling stressed after work, relaxed on a weekend, or during social downtime) influence your willingness to try a new body wash or engage with ads for personal care products? When I'm relaxed, like on a weekend or after a fun outing, I'm more open to new stuff. I might click on ads or browse Nykaa for body washes, especially if they claim to be natural or have good reviews. But on a hectic workday, I just ignore ads and stick to my Dove without thinking twice.
nodes.predictive_value: Advertising or new product discovery attempts are more likely to succeed when this person is in a relaxed, positive mood (e.g., weekends) rather than during stressful workdays.

nodes.label: natural-packaging-aesthetic-preference
nodes.description: Prefers packaging that looks 'Natural,' and in their ideal body wash wants 'simple minimal packaging with ingredients clearly listed.'
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Do you prefer packaging that looks: Natural
[REAL] If you could design your PERFECT body wash from scratch, describe it. ... simple minimal packaging with ingredients clearly listed

[SYNTHETIC] Imagine two body washes are identical in ingredients and fragrance. One is packaged in a sleek, minimalist bottle and priced at ₹399. The other is in a basic plastic bottle priced at ₹249. Both are 250ml. Which do you buy? Buy the basic plastic bottle at ₹249. Packaging looks don’t justify ₹150 extra.
nodes.predictive_value: Will be drawn to packaging with a natural, minimalist aesthetic but will not pay a significant premium for it; functionality and clear information are more important.

nodes.label: sustainability-interest-without-premium
nodes.description: Interested in sustainable packaging (refill bags, big sustainable bottle) in their ideal product, but is not willing to pay a premium for it (0% premium).
evidence_quotes: [REAL] If you could design your PERFECT body wash from scratch, describe it. Big sustainable bottle with refill bags...
[REAL] What is the maximum premium (%) you would pay for a product that aligns with your values? 'Sustainable/eco-friendly packaging':'0'

[SYNTHETIC] You see a body wash at ₹349 that claims '100% recyclable packaging' and donates ₹10 per bottle to ocean cleanup. A nearly identical body wash without these claims costs ₹269. How much of the ₹80 difference do you feel is justified? ₹0 — these claims don't influence what I'm willing to pay
nodes.predictive_value: Will appreciate sustainability claims but will not choose a more expensive product solely for them; sustainable options must be price-competitive.

nodes.label: trusts-honest-influencers
nodes.description: Trusts influencers like Ankush Bahuguna because they are 'honest, genuine, uses few products so clearly not PR, very honest reviews.'
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Who do you follow or trust in the personal care space on social media? Name specific ones and what makes you trust them. Ankush Bahuguna (only name recalled), mostly friends and family
[REAL] What is it about Ankush Bahuguna that makes you trust his opinions on personal care products? Honest, genuine, uses few products so clearly not PR, very honest reviews

[SYNTHETIC] Two influencers recommend different body washes. Influencer A is a skincare expert with 50K followers who gives detailed ingredient analysis. Influencer B is a lifestyle creator with 2M followers who shows the product in their shower routine. Whose recommendation are you more likely to act on? Influencer A, the skincare expert with detailed ingredient analysis.
nodes.predictive_value: Will consider a product recommended by an influencer perceived as genuine and expert-focused (like a skincare expert) over a lifestyle creator, especially if the review includes ingredient analysis.

nodes.label: multi-channel-shopper-prefers-amazon-nykaa
nodes.description: Shops for personal care on Amazon, quick commerce, and Nykaa, but prefers Amazon and Nykaa for their selection and reviews.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Where do you most often buy personal care products? Pick your top 3: ['amazon','quick_commerce','nykaa']

[SYNTHETIC] You're browsing body wash on Nykaa and find a product has 12,000+ ratings with 4.3 stars, while another has only 180 ratings but 4.8 stars and glowing detailed reviews. Price is the same. Which do you pick? Pick the one with 12,000+ ratings and 4.3 stars.
[SYNTHETIC] If you could only buy body wash from ONE channel for the entire next year, which would you choose: (a) Amazon/Flipkart
nodes.predictive_value: Will most likely purchase body wash from Amazon or Nykaa after reading reviews, even if quick commerce is faster, due to trust in the platform's review ecosystem.

nodes.label: quick-commerce-for-urgency-only
nodes.description: Uses quick commerce (Blinkit, Zepto) for immediate needs when almost out, but will go to other platforms if selection is limited or for research.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Do you use quick commerce (Blinkit, Zepto, Swiggy Instamart) for personal care? If yes, does the limited selection change what you buy? Yes, use quick commerce. If limited selection, look on Amazon or Nykaa

[SYNTHETIC] Your regular body wash costs ₹299. During Amazon Great Indian Festival, it's available at ₹199, but delivery will take 5 days. On Blinkit, it's available at full price ₹299 with 10-minute delivery and you're almost out. What do you do? Buy from Blinkit at ₹299 for immediate need. Can't wait 5 days when almost out.

[SYNTHETIC] You're in a rush and ordering body wash on Zepto/Blinkit for 10-minute delivery. Your usual brand isn't available. You see three alternatives you've vaguely heard of. How do you choose? Pick the one closest to your usual brand's description (e.g., moisturizing, gel-based).
nodes.predictive_value: In a stock-out emergency, will pay a premium for quick delivery of a familiar or similar product, but for planned purchases, will avoid quick commerce's limited selection.

nodes.label: expert-and-ingredient-content-responsive
nodes.description: More likely to try a product based on 'expert_review' and 'ingredient_breakdown' content, valuing clinical and factual information.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] What type of content makes you most likely to try a new product? Pick top 2: ['Expert_review','ingredient_breakdown']

[BRANCH] When scrolling through Instagram, you see two ads for a new body wash. Ad A is a 15-second video with a heartwarming story... Ad B is a carousel post listing key ingredients (pH-balanced, sulfate-free), clinical test results for moisturization, and a clip from a dermatologist. Which ad are you more likely to stop and watch fully, and why? I'd definitely stop for Ad B. I need to see the facts – ingredients like pH-balanced and sulfate-free tell me it's safe and effective for my skin.

[SYNTHETIC] You're comparing two body washes on Amazon India. One listing has a detailed ingredient breakdown with percentages, the other has a 2-minute brand video showing the product in use. If you could only view one before deciding, which do you choose? Choose the detailed ingredient breakdown with percentages
nodes.predictive_value: An ad or product page featuring dermatologist clips, ingredient breakdowns, or clinical test data will capture their attention and increase perceived credibility more than an emotional story.

nodes.label: claims-verifier
nodes.description: Checks ingredient lists to verify claims like 'paraben-free' and researches discrepancies rather than taking marketing at face value.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] You notice your body wash has 'Paraben-Free' printed on it. What would you most likely do? (B) Flip the bottle to verify in the ingredient list

[SYNTHETIC] You notice that a body wash's Amazon listing says 'Sulphate Free' but the ingredient list includes 'Sodium C14-16 Olefin Sulfonate' — a mild surfactant that some consider a sulphate variant. Would you ... (E) research the ingredient to understand the distinction?

[SYNTHETIC] You read a Reddit India thread where someone with a similar skin concern shares their body wash routine with before/after photos over 3 months. Versus a brand's clinical trial claim of '90% users saw improvement in 2 weeks.' Which influences your purchase decision more? The Reddit user's detailed before/after photos over 3 months.
nodes.predictive_value: Will independently verify bold marketing claims by reading ingredient lists and seeking out long-term user experiences, making them skeptical of short-term clinical claims.

nodes.label: cautious-about-new-ingredients
nodes.description: Researches new or unfamiliar ingredients (like probiotics, bakuchiol) before trying and seeks expert validation, indicating a need for safety assurance.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] A new body wash uses 'bakuchiol' — a plant-based retinol alternative — as its hero ingredient. You've heard of retinol for face care but never in a body wash. It's ₹449 from a reputable Indian D2C brand. What's your primary hesitation? (A) I don't know if retinol-like ingredients are safe for full-body use

[SYNTHETIC] Blinkit sends you a notification: 'New launch! Probiotic Body Wash with live cultures for skin microbiome health — ₹499.' You've never heard of probiotics in body wash. Rank your likely reactions: (4) Confused — would need a dermatologist's recommendation.

[SYNTHETIC] You scan a body wash barcode using a product-scanning app (like Think Dirty or Yuka) and it rates the product 4/10 for ingredient safety. But you've used it for a year with no issues. Do you (B) research the specific flagged ingredients
nodes.predictive_value: Will avoid products with trendy, unfamiliar ingredients unless accompanied by clear, expert-led explanations of their safety and efficacy for body use.

nodes.label: prefers-established-brands-over-new-D2C
nodes.description: Trusts brands that have been around for a long time (like Dove, used since childhood) and is skeptical of new, fancy brands that 'don't really work.'
evidence_quotes: [REAL] What body wash brands can you name off the top of your head? List as many as you can. Fiama, Plum, Bath & Body Works, Body Shop, Dove, Pears
[REAL] How would you categorize body washes in your mind? What makes one different from another? Has chemicals, harsh on skin, unnecessarily expensive, fancy but doesn't really work

[SYNTHETIC] You're choosing between two body washes at a store. Brand A has been in India for 20 years and your parents used it. Brand B launched 6 months ago, has won 'Best New Launch' at a beauty award... Both cost ₹299. Which do you buy? Buy Brand A — trusted, used by parents, proven track record.
nodes.predictive_value: Will show inherent bias against new D2C or 'fancy' brands, favoring legacy brands with a long history of use and perceived reliability.

nodes.label: not-swayed-by-advertising-or-celebrity-endorsements
nodes.description: Does not trust TV advertisements (rating 1) or claims on packaging (rating 1), and is not influenced by celebrity endorsements.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] How much do you trust the following sources for personal care product information? Rate each 1-5: {'TV advertisements':1,'Claims written on the product packaging':1}

[SYNTHETIC] A new body wash brand launches with a Bollywood celebrity you admire as the face. The product has no reviews yet and costs ₹349 for 250ml. Would you: (B) Wait for online reviews before trying.

[SYNTHETIC] A body wash brand launches a campaign featuring a Bollywood celebrity you admire versus a campaign featuring real Indian dermatologists explaining the science. Same product, same price. Which campaign makes you more likely to buy? (B) The dermatologist campaign — I trust expert credibility more.
nodes.predictive_value: Celebrity-driven ads or emotional storytelling will have minimal impact; this person will seek factual, expert-led information instead.

nodes.label: values-past-experience-highly
nodes.description: Allocates high importance to past experience when making purchase decisions, often repurchasing what has worked.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] Allocate 100 points across these five factors based on what actually drives your final body wash purchase decision at the moment of clicking 'Buy' or picking it off the shelf: 1) Price/deal available right now 2) Past experience with the product 3) How the bottle/packaging looks 4) Specific skin benefit promised 5) Brand name recognition ... Price 30, Past experience 40, Packaging 5, Skin benefit 15, Brand recognition 10.
[REAL] What do you like MOST about your current body wash? (Top 1-2 things) Lightweight, can use on face, cleans effectively, mild, been using since childhood
nodes.predictive_value: A negative experience with a new product will quickly drive them back to their trusted brand, making trial difficult to convert into long-term adoption.

nodes.label: sample-trial-before-commitment
nodes.description: Prefers to try samples, sachets, or travel sizes before committing to a full-size product, especially for new brands.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] What would make you switch away from your current body wash? Pick your top 3: ['free_sample']

[SYNTHETIC] A body wash brand offers two purchase options: (A) Full-size 250ml bottle for ₹349 with no returns, (B) A 50ml trial for ₹99 (higher per-ml cost) with a ₹99 credit toward the full size if you like it. Which option do you choose? A 50ml trial for ₹99 with credit toward full size.

[SYNTHETIC] You discover a body wash you've been curious about is available as a ₹10 sachet at your local kirana store. The same brand's full bottle costs ₹399 on Nykaa. Would you: (A) Buy the sachet to try — perfect low-risk trial
nodes.predictive_value: Will be much more likely to try a new brand if a low-cost, low-commitment sample is available, reducing the perceived risk of wasting money.

nodes.label: not-interested-in-gimmicky-features
nodes.description: Not interested in novel but unnecessary features like color-changing formulas, excessive packaging, or tech integrations; prefers straightforward, effective products.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] You discover a body wash that changes color based on your skin's pH when you apply it — blue means balanced, pink means dry. It's from a brand you've never heard of, costs ₹549, and is only sold on Nykaa. Would you buy it primarily for: (C) You wouldn't buy it — sounds like a gimmick

[SYNTHETIC] A body wash brand lets you customize your fragrance and skin benefit for ₹599 (vs their standard product at ₹349). The customized version comes with your name printed on the bottle. Would you pay the extra ₹250? No, ₹250 extra for customization and name is not worth it.
[REAL] If you could design your PERFECT body wash from scratch, describe it. ... simple minimal packaging
nodes.predictive_value: Will reject products that emphasize novelty or customization over core benefits like moisturization and ingredient safety, seeing them as marketing distractions.

nodes.label: dermatologist-recommendation-responsive
nodes.description: While initially rating trust in dermatologists as 2, their behavior in scenarios shows they value and would act on a dermatologist's recommendation, especially if detailed.
evidence_quotes: [BRANCH] Rank these five purchase triggers from most to least likely to make you buy a body wash you haven't tried before: ... Dermatologist recommendation is most important—I always look for clinical backing and safe ingredients.

[SYNTHETIC] You see an Instagram ad for a new body wash featuring a dermatologist explaining its pH-balanced formula. The same day, your close friend casually mentions they love a completely different body wash. You can only buy one this month. Which do you choose? Choose the dermatologist-recommended body wash.
[SYNTHETIC] If you could only get body wash recommendations from ONE source for the next year, which would you choose? A dermatologist you follow on Instagram.
nodes.predictive_value: A clear, credible endorsement from a dermatologist (especially in video format) can overcome their risk aversion and trigger a trial of a new brand.

nodes.label: budget-conscious-for-household-purchases
nodes.description: When buying for the entire family, prioritizes cost and chooses budget options to stay within a set monthly budget, even if it means compromising personal preference.
evidence_quotes: [SYNTHETIC] You're buying body wash for your entire family (4 members, joint family). Your parents prefer a traditional soap-like body wash at ₹149, but you want a premium one at ₹399 for yourself. The monthly budget for bathing products is ₹600. How do you allocate? (A) Buy the ₹149 one for everyone to stay in budget
[SYNTHETIC] You're buying body wash for your entire family's monthly supply (parents, siblings). You have ₹1,500. How would you allocate? (A) Buy one brand/variant for everyone to keep it simple.
nodes.predictive_value: In a household shopping context, will suppress their own premium preferences to opt for a single, affordable option that satisfies the group and stays within budget.

nodes.label: personal-product-boundary-setter
nodes.description: Prefers to keep personal care products separate, does not like sharing body wash with others, and maintains a personal bathroom stash.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Do other household members use the same body wash as you? Who decides what to buy? I decide what to buy; don't know what others use
[REAL] Do you ever notice if the body wash gets used up faster than expected, suggesting others might be using it too? Only I use my bottle in my personal bathroom

[SYNTHETIC] Your roommate orders a body wash on Amazon that arrives damaged. ... They suggest splitting the cost of a ₹499 'premium' body wash together. Do you: (B) Decline — I don't want to share my body wash
nodes.predictive_value: Will resist communal product sharing arrangements and is likely to purchase separate products for themselves, even within a shared household.

nodes.label: fragrance-appreciator-not-deal-breaker
nodes.description: Prefers floral and ocean aquatic scents, wants fragrance to last all day, but in their ideal product says 'fragrance not important,' indicating it's a nice-to-have, not a primary driver.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] What fragrance families do you naturally gravitate toward? Pick your top 2: Floral,ocean_aquatic
[REAL] How long should a body wash fragrance last on your skin? All_day
[REAL] If you could design your PERFECT body wash from scratch, describe it. ... fragrance not important...
nodes.predictive_value: A pleasant, long-lasting fragrance will enhance their perception of a body wash, but they will not choose a product solely for its scent if it lacks their core requirements (ingredient safety, moisturization).

nodes.label: convenience-driven-in-format-choice
nodes.description: Uses body wash over bar soap because it's 'easy to use,' and chooses purchasing channels based on convenience and speed when needed.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Do you currently use body wash, bar soap, or both? What drives that choice? Only body wash Ñ easy to use

[SYNTHETIC] You're about to run out of body wash tonight. You can either order from Blinkit and get a limited selection delivered in 15 minutes, or order from Amazon and get your preferred brand delivered tomorrow. Which do you choose? Order from Amazon and get my preferred brand tomorrow.

[SYNTHETIC] You're browsing body wash on Nykaa and find a brand you like, but you notice the same product is ₹50 cheaper at your local DMart. Would you drive to DMart to save ₹50, or buy it on Nykaa right now? Buy it on Nykaa right now for convenience.
nodes.predictive_value: Will favor the most convenient format (liquid body wash) and purchasing channel at the moment of need, sometimes prioritizing convenience over immediate cost savings.

nodes.label: India-specific-problem-solver
nodes.description: Highly interested (rating 5) in a body wash specifically formulated for 'Indian skin types and hard water conditions,' expecting it to be lightweight, mild, and moisturizing.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Would you be interested in a body wash specifically formulated for Indian skin types and hard water conditions? {'rating':5,'explanation':'yes'}
[REAL] What specific benefits or features would you expect from a body wash designed for Indian skin and hard water? Lightweight, mild, rinses off easily, moisturizes, cleans, gentle on skin
[REAL] What claims or product descriptions on a body wash would make you stop and read more? Pick top 3: ['india_specific','intense_moisture','natural']
nodes.predictive_value: Will be immediately attracted to and likely to try a body wash that explicitly claims to be designed for Indian skin and hard water, as it directly addresses their perceived needs.

nodes.label: dove-anchored-loyalist
nodes.description: Has used only Dove body wash in the past three months, is very satisfied with it, and evaluates all new concepts against this trusted baseline.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Which body wash or shower gel brands have you used in the past 3 months? Select all that apply. A: ["Dove"]
[SURVEY] Overall, how satisfied are you with the body wash or shower gel you currently use most often? A: Very satisfied
[SURVEY] What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove 60-Second Body Spray? Select all that apply. A: ["Happy with current product"]
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a non-Dove brand concept, this person would likely dismiss it quickly, requiring much stronger proof of benefit to even consider switching.

nodes.label: proven-benefit-skeptic
nodes.description: Exhibits consistent skepticism toward new product claims, requiring external validation (like dermatologist endorsement) before believing they 'work as claimed.'
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove 60-Second Body Spray? Select all that apply. A: ["Might not work as claimed"]
[SURVEY] What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove 60-Second Body Spray concept? A: I would want clearer proof or dermatologist endorsement to trust the 60-second cleaning claim
[SURVEY] What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove Skin ID? Select all that apply. A: ["Don't trust new products"]
nodes.predictive_value: When shown an ad with a bold new functional claim (e.g., 'instantly firms skin'), this person would likely disbelieve it unless it's backed by a trusted third-party seal or clinical data.

nodes.label: value-anchored-price-sensitivity
nodes.description: Consistently cites price as a primary concern for trying new products, but is anchored to the value of their current Dove product rather than seeking the absolute cheapest option.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove 60-Second Body Spray? Select all that apply. A: ["Price might be too high"]
[SURVEY] What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove Skip concept? A: ... by offering a smaller, more affordable trial size to encourage trying it.
[SURVEY] What would you expect to pay for your most preferred concept? (Enter amount in rupees) A: 199
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a premium-priced concept (e.g., over 300 INR), this person would likely reject it immediately unless it demonstrably solves a major, unmet need their current product doesn't address.

nodes.label: moisturization-utility-maximizer
nodes.description: Prioritizes moisturization and skin hydration (rating it 5/5 in importance) and seeks efficient ways to achieve it without adding steps or heavy/greasy feel.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] How important are each of these factors when choosing a body wash? A: {"Moisturization / skin hydration": 5}
[SURVEY] What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove Skip concept? A: ...I often skip lotion, and this would save time while addressing my dry skin.
[SURVEY] What, if anything, would you most like to see improved in body wash or shower gel products? A: I would like body washes to improve moisturization without feeling heavy or greasy
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a concept emphasizing deep, 24-hour moisturization in a lightweight format, this person would likely show high initial interest and click for more details.

nodes.label: routine-integration-evaluator
nodes.description: Assesses new products primarily through the lens of how well they fit into and improve an existing, satisfactory daily routine, valuing time-saving and convenience.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] How well does the Dove 60-Second Body Spray concept fit with your current routine? A: 3
[SURVEY] How likely would the Dove Skip be to save you time in your routine? A: 4
[SURVEY] What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove 60-Second Body Spray concept? A: ...which fits well with busy days when I have little time to shower.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a product requiring a significant routine change (e.g., a separate pre-shower step), this person would likely deem it irrelevant and not consider purchase.

nodes.label: gentleness-and-safety-prioritizer
nodes.description: Rates gentleness on skin as a 5/5 importance factor and expresses specific concern about skin irritation from new products with complex ingredients or fragrances.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] How important are each of these factors when choosing a body wash? A: {"Gentleness on skin": 5}
[SURVEY] What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove Yours & Mine? Select all that apply. A: ["Could irritate my skin"]
[SURVEY] What, if anything, would you most like to see improved in body wash or shower gel products? A: ...to have simpler, more transparent ingredient lists that avoid harsh chemicals.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a concept featuring 'exfoliating beads' or 'intense fragrance,' this person would likely express concern and seek reassurance about gentleness before considering it.

nodes.label: subtle-fragrance-preference
nodes.description: Explicitly desires fragrances to be 'subtle and fresh' and not 'too strong or sweet,' indicating a dislike for overpowering or cloying scents.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove Night Wash concept? A: I would want the fragrance to be subtle and fresh rather than too strong or sweet
[SURVEY] What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove Yours & Mine concept? A: I would make the fragrance subtler and fresher, not too strong or sweet
nodes.predictive_value: When shown an ad emphasizing 'bold, long-lasting fragrance' or 'exotic floral notes,' this person would likely perceive it as a negative and be less inclined to purchase.

nodes.label: cautious-personalization-curiosity
nodes.description: Is intellectually drawn to personalized concepts (like Skin ID) for their relevance, but high skepticism and price sensitivity severely limit actual purchase intent.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove Skin ID concept? A: ...personalized approach using AI skin analysis to address my specific and varying skin needs...
[SURVEY] How would you describe the Dove Skin ID? Rate each pair of characteristics. A: {"Not for me-Made for me": 4, "Old-fashioned-Innovative": 5}
[SURVEY] What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove Skin ID concept? A: I would want the price to be lower and clearer proof that the AI analysis and custom formula actually work
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a personalized skincare quiz, this person would likely engage out of curiosity but abandon the process at the point of purchase if the price is premium or proof is lacking.

nodes.label: emotional-benefit-openness-with-pragmatic-conditions
nodes.description: Acknowledges appeal of emotional benefits (like connection with a partner or stress relief), but this appeal is secondary to practical concerns of price, gentleness, and subtle fragrance.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove Yours & Mine concept? A: ...adds a meaningful emotional touch.
[SURVEY] What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove Night Wash concept? A: ...could make my night shower feel more relaxing and different from the morning.
[SURVEY] What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove Yours & Mine concept? A: ...reduce the price to be more affordable since I am satisfied with my current Dove body wash.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown an ad focusing solely on emotional storytelling without clear proof of functional benefit and value, this person would appreciate it but remain a 'might or might not buy' prospect.

nodes.label: time-compression-opportunity-seeker
nodes.description: Positively evaluates products that promise to save time or compress steps (like 60-second cleanse or skipping lotion), especially for busy days.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove 60-Second Body Spray concept? A: The promise of a quick, effective cleanse in just 60 seconds, which fits well with busy days
[SURVEY] How likely would the Dove Skip be to save you time in your routine? A: 4
[SURVEY] Rank them from most to least appealing. A: ["Dove Skip", "Dove 60-Second Body Spray", ...]
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a product concept positioned around 'multi-tasking' or 'shower efficiency,' this person would likely rank it higher in initial appeal than a purely sensorial or luxury concept.

nodes.label: low-innovation-threshold-excitement
nodes.description: Rates innovation importance only 3/5 and finds most new concepts only 'somewhat unique' and 'somewhat believable,' showing a high bar for genuine excitement.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] How important are each of these factors when choosing a body wash? A: {"Innovation / uniqueness": 3}
[SURVEY] How unique or different is the Dove Skip compared to other body wash products available today? A: Somewhat unique
[SURVEY] How believable are the claims made about the Dove Skin ID? A: Somewhat believable
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a product labeled as 'revolutionary' or 'groundbreaking,' this person would likely default to skepticism and require very concrete, demystified explanations of the benefit.

nodes.label: cleansing-effectiveness-non-negotiable
nodes.description: Views cleansing effectiveness as a 5/5 importance table-stake; any new product must first and foremost deliver on this basic function.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] How important are each of these factors when choosing a body wash? A: {"Cleansing effectiveness": 5}
[SURVEY] What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove 60-Second Body Spray concept? A: The promise of a quick, effective cleanse...
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a concept that emphasizes novel benefits (e.g., mood-enhancing) but doesn't prominently reaffirm core cleansing power, this person would likely question its fundamental utility.

nodes.label: moderate-brand-fit-expectation
nodes.description: Expects Dove products to be trustworthy and gentle, rating all new Dove concepts as fitting 'somewhat well' with the brand, showing a stable but not rigid brand schema.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] How well does the Dove 60-Second Body Spray idea fit with what you expect from the Dove brand? A: Fits somewhat well
[SURVEY] How well does the Dove Skip idea fit with what you expect from the Dove brand? A: Fits somewhat well
[SURVEY] How important are each of these factors when choosing a body wash? A: {"Brand trust and reputation": 4}
nodes.predictive_value: When shown an extreme Dove concept (e.g., a highly fragranced, decorative body glitter wash), this person would likely rate brand fit poorly, reducing believability and appeal.

nodes.label: availability-anxiety
nodes.description: Expresses specific concern that a new product 'might not be available where I shop,' indicating a preference for convenient, reliable retail access.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove Yours & Mine? Select all that apply. A: ["Might not be available where I shop"]
nodes.predictive_value: When interested in an online-exclusive product, this person would likely hesitate unless shipping is free/fast and the return policy is very lenient.

nodes.label: trial-size-advocate-for-risk-mitigation
nodes.description: Explicitly suggests smaller, affordable trial sizes as a way to overcome hesitation and encourage trying new, higher-priced products.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove Skip concept? A: ... by offering a smaller, more affordable trial size to encourage trying it.
nodes.predictive_value: When making a purchase decision, the presence of a low-cost trial/sample size would significantly increase this person's likelihood of converting interest into an initial try.

nodes.label: satisfied-routine-anchor
nodes.description: Highly satisfied with current Dove body wash and uses it as a stable anchor in their daily routine, creating resistance to switching.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Overall, how satisfied are you with the body wash or shower gel you currently use most often? A: Very satisfied
[SURVEY] What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove 60-Second Body Spray? A: ["Happy with current product"]
[SURVEY] Which body wash or shower gel brands have you used in the past 3 months? A: ["Dove"]
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a new product concept, this person would likely default to comparing it unfavorably to their current Dove product unless presented with a compelling, proven advantage.

nodes.label: gentle-hydration-maximizer
nodes.description: Prioritizes cleansing effectiveness, moisturization, and gentleness on skin (all rated 5/5) above fragrance or innovation.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] How important are each of these factors when choosing a body wash? A: {"Cleansing effectiveness": 5, "Moisturization / skin hydration": 5, "Gentleness on skin": 5, "Innovation / uniqueness": 3}

[SURVEY] What, if anything, would you most like to see improved in body wash or shower gel products? A: I would like body washes to have gentler ingredients without harsh chemicals, better hydration without needing extra lotion
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a product concept, this person would likely focus first on claims about skin health and hydration, dismissing concepts that prioritize fragrance or gimmicky features over these core benefits.

nodes.label: proof-requiring-skeptic
nodes.description: Consistently questions how well new products work as claimed and seeks clearer evidence before believing marketing promises.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove Skip concept? A: I would want to see... clearer evidence that the moisturization really lasts all day.
[SURVEY] What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove Skin ID concept? A: I would want clearer proof that the AI skin analysis works as claimed
[SURVEY] What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove Skip? A: ["Might not work as claimed"]
nodes.predictive_value: When shown an ad with a performance claim, this person would likely seek out third-party validation, reviews, or clinical study data before considering purchase.

nodes.label: trial-size-gatekeeper
nodes.description: Prefers to test new products through affordable, smaller trial sizes before committing to a full-price purchase, mitigating financial and performance risk.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove 60-Second Body Spray concept? A: possibly offer a more affordable trial size since price is a concern.
[SURVEY] What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove Yours & Mine concept? A: I would improve it by offering a more affordable trial size or smaller kit to try first.
[SURVEY] What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove Skin ID concept? A: possibly a more affordable price or smaller sizes to try first

nodes.label: value-anchored-at-199
nodes.description: Has a strong internal reference price of around ₹199 for body wash, making any concept priced significantly higher (like ₹699-₹999) seem expensive and requiring justification.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] What would you expect to pay for your most preferred concept? (Enter amount in rupees) A: 199

[SURVEY] What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove Skin ID? A: ["Price might be too high"]
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a purchase scenario with a price above ₹250, this person would likely hesitate and mentally calculate cost-per-use or seek discounts, unless the product offers a uniquely proven benefit.

nodes.label: time-saving-utility-seeker
nodes.description: Actively attracted to concepts that promise to save time or steps in their routine, such as faster cleansing or eliminating a separate moisturizing step.

[SURVEY] How likely would the Dove Skip be to save you time in your routine? A: 4
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a product concept positioned around convenience or routine simplification, this person would likely rate it higher on relevance and appeal, provided the time-saving claim is believable.

nodes.label: brand-fit-monitor
nodes.description: Evaluates new product ideas through the lens of whether they 'fit' with the Dove brand's established identity of gentleness and care.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] How well does the Dove 60-Second Body Spray idea fit with what you expect from the Dove brand? A: Fits very well
[SURVEY] How well does the Dove Skip idea fit with what you expect from the Dove brand? A: Fits very well
[SURVEY] How well does the Dove Skin ID idea fit with what you expect from the Dove brand? A: Fits very well
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a new Dove concept that seems gimmicky or overly tech-focused, this person would likely question its fit with the brand, reducing its believability and appeal.

nodes.label: low-olfactory-priority

evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] How important are each of these factors when choosing a body wash? A: {"Fragrance / scent quality": 3}
[SURVEY] What, if anything, would you most like to see improved in body wash or shower gel products? A: a fragrance that is fresh but not overpowering.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown an ad focusing primarily on exotic or strong fragrance notes, this person would likely disengage, preferring messaging that highlights skin-feel or ingredient benefits.

nodes.label: cautious-innovation-appreciator

evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] How unique or different is the Dove Skin ID compared to other body wash products available today? A: Very unique
[SURVEY] How would you describe the Dove Skin ID? A: {"Old-fashioned-Innovative": 5}
[SURVEY] How important are each of these factors when choosing a body wash? A: {"Innovation / uniqueness": 3}
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a highly innovative product concept, this person would likely express intellectual interest but remain in the 'might or might not buy' category until practical benefits and value are clearly demonstrated.

nodes.label: ritual-occasion-openness

evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove Night Wash concept? A: The calming ritual with chamomile and lavender oils to help unwind and prepare for sleep.
[SURVEY] How well does the Dove Night Wash concept fit with your current routine? A: 3
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a product concept tied to a specific self-care moment (e.g., morning energizing, post-workout), this person would likely assess its fit with their actual schedule and the authenticity of its benefit claims.

nodes.label: personal-relevance-filter
nodes.description: Filters all new concepts through a stringent test of personal relevance to their own skin needs and routine, dismissing those perceived as 'not for me'.

[SURVEY] How would you describe the Dove Yours & Mine? A: {"Not for me-Made for me": 2}
[SURVEY] How would you describe the Dove Skip? A: {"Not for me-Made for me": 4}

nodes.label: comparative-rank-driver
nodes.description: Makes final preference decisions by explicitly ranking options (like ranking the 5 concepts) based on a composite of time-saving, relevance, and fit.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Now that you've seen all 5 concepts, please rank them from most to least appealing. Pick your top 3. A: ["Dove 60-Second Body Spray", "Dove Skip", "Dove Night Wash", "Dove Yours & Mine", "Dove Skin ID"]
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a set of purchase options in a retail setting, this person would likely mentally rank them, favoring the one that best balances their core priorities, rather than choosing the most novel or cheapest.

nodes.label: satisfied-dove-loyalist-with-high-switching-barrier
nodes.description: Uses only Dove, is very satisfied with current product, and cites 'happy with current product' as a primary concern for trying any new offering.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] Overall, how satisfied are you with the body wash or shower gel you currently use most often? A: Very satisfied
[REAL] Which body wash or shower gel brands have you used in the past 3 months? Select all that apply. A: ["Dove"]
[REAL] What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove Skip? Select all that apply. A: ["Happy with current product"]
nodes.predictive_value: When shown any new product concept (even from Dove), this person would likely default to their current favorite unless the new concept offers a clear, proven, and affordable solution to an unmet need.

nodes.label: clinical-proof-seeker-for-efficacy-claims
nodes.description: Consistently requests dermatologist endorsements, user testimonials, or clinical proof to believe a product will work as advertised.
evidence_quotes: [REAL] I would want clearer proof that it really works as claimed, maybe some dermatologist endorsement or strong user reviews
[REAL] more evidence that the moisturizing effect lasts as long as claimed, perhaps with user testimonials or clinical proof
[REAL] stronger evidence such as dermatologist endorsements or verified user testimonials to trust the AI analysis and product effectiveness

nodes.label: value-for-money-maximizer-with-specific-price-anchors
nodes.description: Rates 'value for money' as extremely important (5/5), cites price as a consistent concern, and anchors expected prices to specific rupee amounts (₹199, ₹300, ₹500-600).

nodes.label: dry-skin-focused-benefit-prioritizer

nodes.label: pragmatic-time-saver-open-to-routine-efficiency
nodes.description: Finds time-saving benefits like 60-second cleansing or skipping a lotion step 'very appealing' because they fit into a busy schedule, ranking such concepts highest.

nodes.description: Believes time-saving and moisturizing innovations 'fit very well' with Dove's brand, especially when they leverage proven technologies from its existing range (e.g., micellar).

[REAL] the use of proven micellar technology from Dove's face care range
nodes.predictive_value: When Dove launches an extension that logically builds on a trusted, existing product benefit (like moisturization or mild cleansing), this person would likely find it more believable and relevant than a completely novel brand proposition.

nodes.label: skeptical-of-ai-and-hyper-personalization

[REAL] stronger evidence such as dermatologist endorsements or verified user testimonials to trust the AI analysis
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a highly tech-driven, personalized skincare product, this person would likely be intrigued but ultimately hesitant, perceiving it as higher risk and potentially overpriced compared to simpler, proven solutions.

nodes.label: ritual-appreciator-for-specific-occasions

nodes.predictive_value: When shown a product concept framed around enhancing a specific self-care moment (e.g., night, post-workout), this person would likely evaluate it as a potential complement to, not a replacement for, their core daily wash.

nodes.label: low-innovation-thrill-seeker

nodes.label: functional-fragrance-viewer

nodes.predictive_value: When evaluating a line extension from a trusted brand, this person would likely use brand fit as a key filter, rejecting ideas that feel too gimmicky or distant from the brand's established equity.

nodes.description: Finds unique and innovative concepts appealing and exciting, but default purchase intent is 'might or might not buy,' indicating interest is tempered by caution.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: How unique or different is the Dove Skin ID compared to other body wash products available today? A: Very unique
[SURVEY] Q: Based on what you've seen, how likely would you be to buy the Dove Skin ID? A: Might or might not buy
[SURVEY] Q: How would you describe the Dove Skin ID? A: {"Old-fashioned-Innovative": 5}
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a highly innovative concept, this person would likely engage with it positively but require strong proof-points and a low-risk trial option to convert interest into purchase.

nodes.label: proof-and-review-dependent
nodes.description: Expresses skepticism about product claims and states a need for clearer proof or trusted user reviews to believe efficacy.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove 60-Second Body Spray concept? A: I would want clearer proof or trusted user reviews to back the 60-second cleaning claim...
[SURVEY] Q: What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove Skip? A: ["Might not work as claimed"]
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a product making a bold functional claim (e.g., 'works in 60 seconds'), this person would likely be unmoved without seeing third-party validation, testimonials, or clinical study data.

nodes.label: time-saving-utility-seeker
nodes.description: Ranked time-saving concepts (60-Second Body Spray, Skip) as most appealing, indicating a busy lifestyle or desire for routine efficiency.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: Now that you've seen all 5 concepts, please rank them from most to least appealing. A: ["Dove 60-Second Body Spray", "Dove Skip", ...]

[SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove 60-Second Body Spray concept? A: The promise of a quick, effective clean in just 60 seconds, which fits well with busy days...
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a concept that promises to save time or simplify a routine step, this person would likely rate it higher in appeal and relevance than other benefit types.

nodes.label: moderate-price-anchor-~299
nodes.description: Expects to pay around ₹299 for a preferred body wash concept, indicating a specific, mid-range price expectation.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What would you expect to pay for your most preferred concept? (Enter amount in rupees) A: 299
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a premium-priced concept (e.g., over ₹400), this person would likely cite price as a key concern and be less likely to purchase without exceptional justification.

nodes.label: trial-size-advocate-for-risk-reduction
nodes.description: Repeatedly suggests offering more affordable or smaller trial sizes for new concepts to lower the risk of trying.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove 60-Second Body Spray concept? A: ...possibly a more affordable trial size
[SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove Night Wash concept? A: ...possibly offering a smaller size for trial
nodes.predictive_value: When introduced to a new product, this person would be significantly more likely to try it if a low-cost trial/sample size is available before committing to a full-size purchase.

nodes.label: functional-benefit-prioritizer
nodes.description: Rates cleansing effectiveness, moisturization, and gentleness as '5' (extremely important), while fragrance and uniqueness are rated lower ('3').
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: How important are each of these factors when choosing a body wash? A: {"Cleansing effectiveness": 5, "Moisturization / skin hydration": 5, "Fragrance / scent quality": 3, "Gentleness on skin": 5, "Innovation / uniqueness": 3}
nodes.predictive_value: When evaluating a new concept, this person would likely focus first on core functional performance claims (clean, moisturize, be gentle) and be less swayed by novel formats or scent stories alone.

nodes.label: natural-subtle-fragrance-preference
nodes.description: Prefers fragrances that are natural-smelling and subtle, expressing concern about strong or overwhelming scents.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove Night Wash concept? A: I would suggest making the fragrance less strong or more natural-smelling...
[SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, would you change or improve about the Dove Yours & Mine concept? A: ...the fragrance to feel more natural and subtle rather than strong.
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a product advertised with a bold, intense, or synthetic fragrance, this person would likely perceive it negatively and request a milder alternative.

nodes.label: personalization-curious-but-wary
nodes.description: Finds personalized skin analysis (Skin ID) very appealing and unique, but concerns about skin irritation and price keep purchase intent low.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove Skin ID concept? A: The personalized skin analysis that tailors the body wash to my specific skin needs...
[SURVEY] Q: What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove Skin ID? A: ["Could irritate my skin", "Price might be too high"]
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a personalized product, this person would be highly engaged by the premise but would need strong safety guarantees and a compelling value proposition to overcome caution.

nodes.label: routine-fit-evaluator
nodes.description: Consistently rates how well a concept fits their current routine as a '3' (neutral), indicating they evaluate new products through the lens of integration, not disruption.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: How well does the Dove 60-Second Body Spray concept fit with your current routine? A: 3
[SURVEY] Q: How well does the Dove Skip concept fit with your current routine? A: 3
[SURVEY] Q: How well does the Dove Skin ID concept fit with your current routine? A: 3
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a product that requires a significant change in habit (e.g., a multi-step system), this person would likely rate it lower in relevance and be less likely to adopt it.

nodes.label: happy-but-not-closed-to-switch
nodes.description: Despite being very satisfied with current Dove, they consistently list 'happy with current product' as a concern for trying new ones, showing openness is conditional.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: Overall, how satisfied are you with the body wash or shower gel you currently use most often? A: Very satisfied
[SURVEY] Q: What concerns, if any, would you have about trying the Dove 60-Second Body Spray? A: ["Happy with current product"]
nodes.predictive_value: When marketed to, this person needs a clear, superior benefit over their current Dove product to justify the switch; incremental improvements may not be enough.

nodes.label: believability-hurdle-moderate
nodes.description: Finds claims for all new concepts only 'somewhat believable,' never 'very believable,' indicating a default skeptical stance.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: How believable are the claims made about the Dove 60-Second Body Spray? A: Somewhat believable
[SURVEY] Q: How believable are the claims made about the Dove Skin ID? A: Somewhat believable
nodes.predictive_value: Marketing messages that use vague or exaggerated language would likely be discounted; this person responds better to concrete, demonstrable, or scientifically-backed claims.

nodes.label: social-connection-scent-appreciator
nodes.description: Finds appeal in the idea of a shared, personalized fragrance experience that connects them with a partner throughout the day.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove Yours & Mine concept? A: The personalized fragrance quiz and the idea of carrying a subtle scent that connects me with my partner throughout the day is most appealing...
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a product marketed as a shared experience or gift for couples, this person would likely see higher emotional relevance and consider it for specific, relationship-oriented occasions.

nodes.label: relaxation-ritual-receptive
nodes.description: Values the idea of a calming night-time shower ritual with ingredients like chamomile and lavender to aid unwinding.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: What, if anything, did you find most appealing about the Dove Night Wash concept? A: The calming ritual with chamomile and lavender that helps unwind and prepare for sleep is most appealing...
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a product positioned for evening use or stress relief, this person would likely be more interested if it promises a genuine sensory experience that enhances relaxation, not just cleaning.

nodes.label: brand-trust-leverages-openness
nodes.description: Trust in the Dove brand ('fits very well') makes them more receptive to and forgiving of innovative extensions from that brand.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: How well does the Dove 60-Second Body Spray idea fit with what you expect from the Dove brand? A: Fits very well
[SURVEY] Q: Based on what you've seen, how likely would you be to buy the Dove Skip? A: Probably would buy
nodes.predictive_value: An innovative concept from Dove would receive a more favorable initial assessment than the same concept from an unknown or less-trusted brand.

nodes.label: convenience-moderate-priority
nodes.description: Rates convenience/ease of use as a '4' in importance, showing it's valued but secondary to core functional benefits.
evidence_quotes: [SURVEY] Q: How important are each of these factors when choosing a body wash? A: {"Convenience / ease of use": 4}
nodes.predictive_value: When shown a new format (e.g., spray, bar, wipe), this person would appreciate the convenience if it doesn't compromise on cleansing, moisturizing, or gentleness.

links.dimension: Impulse versus deliberate purchase decision-making style
Emotional versus rational advertising engagement preference
Visual versus textual information processing bias
Context-dependent mood and situational receptivity
Social conformity versus individuality in product choice

links.mechanism: Their deep need to verify ingredient safety and efficacy drives them to cross-reference multiple review platforms for authentic user experiences, as brand claims alone are distrusted.

Loyalty to Dove creates tension with price sensitivity; they complain about Dove's price but continue repurchasing due to trust, yet a significant price increase would trigger active searching for cheaper alternatives that match Dove's gentle profile.
Trust in objective authority (doctors) extends to low-risk trial methods (samples); both represent low-emotion, evidence-based approaches to product evaluation, contrasting with influencer hype.

Price sensitivity directly causes rejection of premium packaging costs; they see fancy packaging as an unnecessary expense that doesn't improve the core product function, aligning with their minimalist preference.

Distrust of international brands' credibility aligns with their focus on ingredient safety; they perceive Indian brands as more transparent and appropriately formulated for their concerns (like hard water), reinforcing the preference.

Their dry skin concern directly drives the specific demand for lightweight, non-stripping formulations that moisturize without heaviness, making 'gentle' a functional necessity rather than a preference.

Viewing fragrance as secondary allows primary focus on functional skin benefits (gentleness, moisturization); this hierarchy ensures product selection isn't distracted by scent marketing, keeping core skin needs central.

Their inconsistent routine execution leads to last-minute realizations of running out, making quick commerce a necessary backup despite preferring other channels; mood-driven negligence creates urgent need states.

Both traits stem from an ethical/health-conscious consumer identity that prioritizes safety and non-harm, creating a consistent filter: products must be safe for their body (non-toxic) and ethically produced (cruelty-free).

Trust in close circles likely reinforced the initial adoption of Dove (potentially a family habit), and continued use is validated by not receiving negative feedback from trusted sources, creating a reinforcement loop.

links.dimension: Impulse versus deliberate purchase decision-making style

links.mechanism: Childhood loyalty creates strong inertia, but pragmatic price sensitivity overrides this loyalty when costs increase significantly, triggering active search for alternatives.
Habitual ingredient checking enables them to verify 'natural' claims against their strict toxic-free definition, ensuring alignment between claimed and actual formulation.
High trust in personal networks extends to medical professionals within those networks, making doctor recommendations the most powerful switch trigger despite general loyalty.
Skeptical cross-checking behavior across platforms makes them appreciate influencers who demonstrate similar scrutiny (ingredient analysis, non-promotional tone) versus typical PR-driven content.
Mood-driven openness to grooming enhancement makes them receptive to emotional, fun ads when relaxed, but their underlying rational nature requires proof points before actual purchase.
Urgent need drives quick commerce purchases despite their preference for thorough cross-platform research, creating tension between convenience and their standard information-seeking behavior.
Pre-existing concern for dry skin and gentle formulations drives heightened interest in products specifically designed for Indian skin/hard water conditions that promise better compatibility.

Value calculation extends to sustainability—they'll consider refill systems only if the per-ml cost and convenience match or beat regular bottles, making economic practicality the gate for environmental adoption.
Lack of philosophical allegiance to either clinical or Ayurvedic approaches necessitates self-education about ingredients and certifications to make practical decisions between both claim types.

While they typically separate product performance from brand controversies, animal testing represents an absolute ethical boundary that overrides their usual pragmatic separation of product and brand ethics.
Trust in personal networks extends to trusted online communities like Reddit where they perceive similar authenticity and lack of commercial motive as in friends/family recommendations.

links.mechanism: Long-term emotional and functional attachment to Dove creates a powerful habit and trust, making them ignore or dismiss competing products without compelling reasons to switch.

Their primary focus on reading ingredient lists naturally leads them to scrutinize and prioritize products with certifications like paraben-free and sulfate-free, aligning with their definition of 'natural'.
High trust in social proof and crowd-sourced opinions drives them to invest significant time reading reviews on Amazon, Nykaa, and Reddit before any purchase decision.

While they value quality and have paid up to ₹400, their price sensitivity creates tension with paying more for 'clinical' claims; they demand tangible proof (study results) to justify any price increase.
Trust in Indian brands makes them more receptive to claims of products specifically formulated for Indian conditions, seeing them as more credible and relevant than generic international products.
Their risk-averse trial style reinforces skepticism toward novel, unproven product formats, as they prefer tried-and-tested products validated by masses or experts.

While they appreciate minimal, transparent packaging, they are unwilling to pay extra for sustainable features, so they will choose the cheaper option even if it means forgoing eco-friendly packaging.
Their self-described laziness and mood-dependent routine reduce the mental energy available for product exploration, reinforcing their default loyalty to familiar Dove.
Their skin concern with dryness directly shapes their preference for lightweight, moisturizing formulations that address dryness without feeling heavy or greasy.

links.mechanism: Their focus on dissecting ingredient lists is in service of verifying functional claims (moisturization, gentleness) and avoiding irritants, which reinforces their prioritization of tangible skin benefits over sensory pleasures like fragrance.

Their loyalty to Dove is strong, but it exists within a strict financial boundary. If Dove's price crosses their mental threshold, the price-sensitivity trait overrides loyalty, triggering an active search for alternatives.

Their hierarchical trust model mandates that information from lower-trust sources (ads, brand claims) must be corroborated by higher-trust sources (reviews, friends) before action is taken, which is the essence of their research-heavy, skeptical switching behavior.

Their inherent risk aversion and need for validation before trying anything new directly drives their behavior of spending significant time aggregating and cross-checking reviews from multiple, diverse online sources.

Their bias toward India-specific formulations makes them more receptive to reviews and recommendations from other Indian consumers (who share their skin/water context), further strengthening the weight of community and friend-based information in their trust pyramid.

Their sensitivity to others' perceptions makes doctor recommendations a powerful social proof signal, and free samples provide a risk-free way to trial a product that might be perceived as more premium or effective by their peers.

Their 'slightly lazy' side seeks simplicity and ease, which could favor basic products, but their aspirational 'maximizer' side seeks advanced benefits. This tension is resolved by prioritizing products that promise elevated results (function) without demanding extra steps (compatible with low-mood days).

Their focus on calculating true value (price per ml, direct discount vs. bundled item) is the cognitive tool they use to enforce their strict price anchor and resist paying for perceived marketing fluff.

links.mechanism: Their focus on ingredients is not just about avoidance (toxins) but about understanding efficacy. This drives them to seek expert content (dermatologists, cosmetic chemists) who can translate ingredient lists into meaningful benefits and validate marketing claims, creating a research loop.

Deep-seated trust in Dove, built over years of satisfactory use, creates a high barrier to switching. This directly causes their risk aversion, making them demand extremely low-risk trial mechanisms (samples, small sizes) before even considering a new brand that must first prove it can match or exceed Dove's reliable performance.

Their fundamental trust in peer experiences extends beyond their immediate circle to the digital 'crowd.' They see aggregated online reviews as an extension of 'friends and family' recommendations, providing a broader base of social proof to validate a product before they personally risk trying it.

There is a constant tension between their idealized self (who enjoys thorough grooming) and their actual 'slightly lazy' behavior. This contradiction makes them particularly susceptible to products that promise to deliver the benefits of an extensive routine (e.g., exfoliation, deep moisture) through a simple, single-step product, resolving the internal conflict.

Their willingness to pay is strictly tied to proven, functional value. This makes them highly skeptical of features that don't contribute to core efficacy (cleansing, moisturizing, safety). They view gimmicks and seasonal variants as unjustified price inflations, reinforcing their price sensitivity in those contexts.

Their bias towards Indian brands is not purely patriotic; it's linked to a perception that Indian brands inherently understand and formulate for local needs (skin, water, climate). The 'India-specific' claim powerfully activates this preference, as it aligns with their belief that local solutions are more credible and effective for them.

Their need for validated information causes them to be highly discerning about influencer credibility. They filter out influencers who seem like paid promoters (lacking credibility) and gravitate towards those who demonstrate expertise and unbiased honesty, as these are the only ones who can satisfy their need for trustworthy validation.

While they have specific and strong fragrance preferences, they also state 'fragrance not important' in their perfect product description and are drawn to 'natural' minimalism. This suggests fragrance is a 'hygiene factor'—it must meet their quality bar (fresh, long-lasting) but isn't a primary driver of value worthy of a premium, creating a nuanced stance where good fragrance is expected but not extravagantly paid for.

links.mechanism: Because they prioritize ingredients above all else, they have developed a browsing habit of going straight to the ingredient list and reviews, skipping marketing fluff.
Their strong loyalty creates inertia, making them resistant to switch, but these two specific triggers (expert validation and risk-free trial) are powerful enough to overcome that loyalty.

Their trust in external validation extends to trusting expert reviews and detailed ingredient analyses, which they see as reliable sources of information akin to trusted friends or credible crowd-sourced reviews.
Their defined price comfort zone and sensitivity mean they are unwilling to stretch their budget for added-value claims like sustainability or Ayurvedic, seeing them as non-essential extras.

Their fun-loving, mood-driven personality makes them receptive to emotional, uplifting ads in the right moment, but their underlying need for safety and efficacy requires rational proof to convert that attention into consideration.
Their preference for the familiar makes them risk-averse toward new brands, leading them to only try new products when the risk is eliminated (free sample) or minimized (small trial size).
Their general trust in experts makes them selectively trust influencers who demonstrate expert-like qualities (honesty, ingredient focus, lack of commercial bias), while rejecting mainstream hype.

Their channel choice is driven by context: quick commerce serves the functional need for speed when out of product, but its limited selection and lack of review depth make it unsuitable for the discovery and research they require for new products.

links.mechanism: Their strong loyalty to familiar brands creates a high barrier to entry for new products. This barrier is only lowered by mechanisms that significantly reduce perceived risk, such as free samples or strong guarantees, aligning their loyalty with their risk aversion.

Their fundamental need to verify safety and efficacy (ingredients-first) directly drives their behavior of cross-referencing brand claims with multiple independent review sources, especially seeking out negative feedback to balance potentially biased positive reviews.

The deep-seated trust placed in their immediate social circle (friends/family) over impersonal advertising causes them to weight a close friend's recommendation more heavily than any other marketing claim, including those from experts or influencers.

There is a tension between their desire for safe, 'natural' (non-toxic) ingredients—which often command a premium—and their unwillingness to pay extra for values-based claims (sustainability, Ayurvedic). They resolve this by seeking value within the 'natural' space, preferring Indian brands that offer the safety they seek at an 'appropriate price.'

Their fun-loving personality and aesthetic preferences can initially draw them to visually appealing, emotional ads, but their rational, research-oriented side quickly takes over. The engagement is emotional, but the decision is analytical, creating a two-step process where packaging/ads get attention, but ingredients and reviews close the sale.

Price sensitivity motivates their behavior of checking multiple channels (Amazon vs. Nykaa vs. quick commerce) to find the best deal for a given situation (urgency vs. planned purchase). Their channel agility is a direct tool for optimizing cost.
